text: A 67-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe chest pain that radiates to his back. The pain started while resting. Blood pressure is 190/100 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the BACK + hypertension = Aortic dissection. BP difference between arms is classic. Dx: CT angiography. Do NOT give anticoagulants.

text: A 4-year-old child presents with a barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and mild respiratory distress that is worse at night. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis): barking (seal-like) cough + stridor + hoarseness. Caused by parainfluenza virus. Tx: Dexamethasone + racemic epinephrine if severe.

text: A 22-year-old male presents with sudden severe testicular pain while playing soccer. The testicle is high-riding and the cremasteric reflex is absent. What is the next best step?
rationale: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. Do NOT wait for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high. 6-hour window for salvage. Absent cremasteric reflex is most reliable sign.

text: A patient presents with irregularly irregular pulse, palpitations, and fatigue. ECG shows no identifiable P waves. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves. Most feared complication = embolic stroke. Manage with rate control + anticoagulation (DOAC preferred).

text: A 50-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe headache he describes as the worst headache of his life. He was exercising at the time. Neurologic exam is normal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Thunderclap headache = worst headache of life = SAH until proven otherwise. Dx: CT head first → if negative → LP for xanthochromia. Cause: ruptured berry aneurysm.

text: A 45-year-old obese woman presents with right upper quadrant pain after eating a fatty meal, fever, and nausea. Murphy sign is positive. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute cholecystitis: RUQ pain + fever + positive Murphy sign + triggered by fatty meal. Dx: Ultrasound (gallstones + thickened GB wall). Tx: IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, cholecystectomy.

text: A 5-year-old child presents with high fever, drooling, difficulty swallowing, and inspiratory stridor. He is sitting in a tripod position and looks toxic. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis: drooling + dysphagia + toxic appearance + tripod position. MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Secure airway FIRST. Do NOT examine throat. IV antibiotics after airway secured.

text: A 35-year-old man presents with sudden severe flank pain radiating to the groin, hematuria, and nausea. The pain is colicky. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Ureteric stone: classic colicky flank pain radiating to groin + hematuria. Dx: CT KUB (non-contrast). Tx: analgesia (NSAIDs) + IV fluids + tamsulosin. Stones <5mm usually pass spontaneously.

text: A 4-week-old male infant presents with projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feeding. He appears hungry after vomiting. A olive-shaped mass is palpable in the epigastrium. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pyloric stenosis: projectile non-bilious vomiting at 2-6 weeks + olive mass + metabolic alkalosis (hypochloremic). More common in firstborn males. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

text: A 54-year-old man presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. Serum lipase is elevated 3x upper limit of normal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back + lipase >3x ULN. Causes: Gallstones #1, Alcohol #2 (GET SMASHED). Tx: IV fluids first, NPO, analgesia.

text: A 68-year-old man presents with painless progressive jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and 12 kg weight loss over 3 months. He has no abdominal pain. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Painless jaundice + weight loss in elderly = pancreatic head cancer until proven otherwise. Courvoisier sign: palpable non-tender gallbladder. Dx: CT abdomen. CA 19-9 elevated.

text: A 40-year-old woman presents with symmetric joint pain and swelling involving MCP and PIP joints, with morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes. RF and anti-CCP are positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: RA: symmetric MCP/PIP (not DIP) + morning stiffness >1h + RF/anti-CCP positive. Tx: Methotrexate (DMARD) first-line. NSAIDs for symptoms. Anti-CCP is most specific.

text: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling of the first metatarsophalangeal joint (big toe) that started overnight. Uric acid is elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale: Gout: podagra (first MTP joint) + hyperuricemia + negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Acute tx: NSAIDs or colchicine. Prevention: allopurinol (urate-lowering therapy).

text: A 30-year-old woman presents with weight loss, palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, and diarrhea. Examination shows exophthalmos and diffuse goiter. Diagnosis?
rationale: Graves disease: most common cause of hyperthyroidism. Exophthalmos is pathognomonic. Low TSH + high T3/T4. Tx: Methimazole (first-line), radioactive iodine, or surgery.

text: A 45-year-old man presents with central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, purple abdominal striae, and hypertension. Fasting glucose is elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale: Cushing syndrome: excess cortisol → moon face + central obesity + purple striae + HTN + hyperglycemia. Most common cause: exogenous steroids. Endogenous: pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease).

text: A 35-year-old woman has persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating for 6 weeks. She denies suicidal ideation. First-line treatment?
rationale: MDD first-line: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine). Allow 4-6 weeks for response. Treat minimum 6-9 months after remission. Benzodiazepines not first-line.

text: A 20-year-old man presents with 8-month history of auditory hallucinations, believing he is being controlled by aliens, social withdrawal, and flat affect. Diagnosis?
rationale: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) + negative symptoms (flat affect, withdrawal) >6 months. Tx: atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine). Clozapine for treatment-resistant.

text: A 9-month-old infant presents with episodic severe crampy abdominal pain, drawing up legs, and vomiting. Stool is described as currant jelly. Abdomen is distended. Diagnosis?
rationale: Intussusception: classic triad = colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stool (blood + mucus). Most common 6mo-3yr. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Air enema reduction (if no perforation), surgery if failed.

text: A 60-year-old man presents with black tarry stools (melena) and lightheadedness. He takes ibuprofen daily for arthritis. Most likely source of bleeding?
rationale: Melena = digested blood = upper GI source (above ligament of Treitz). NSAID use → peptic ulcer = #1 cause of upper GI bleeding. Dx: Urgent endoscopy. Tx: PPI + endoscopic hemostasis.

text: A 72-year-old woman develops sudden right-sided weakness, right facial droop, and inability to speak. Symptoms started 2 hours ago. What is the most appropriate first investigation?
rationale: Acute stroke: CT head NON-CONTRAST first to exclude hemorrhage before tPA. MRI more sensitive but slower. Time is brain - door-to-needle <60 min. tPA window: 4.5 hours.

rationale.text: A 70-year-old man presents with sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back. BP is 200/100 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Aortic dissection: tearing pain radiating to BACK + hypertension. BP difference between arms. Dx: CT angiography. Do NOT anticoagulate.

rationale.text: A 4-year-old child presents with a barking seal-like cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor worse at night. Afebrile. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Croup (parainfluenza virus): barking cough + stridor + hoarseness. Worse at night. Tx: Dexamethasone ± racemic epinephrine if severe.

rationale.text: A patient presents with palpitations and an irregularly irregular pulse. ECG shows no discernible P waves. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves. Most common arrhythmia. Biggest risk = stroke. Tx: rate control + anticoagulation (DOAC).

rationale.text: A 20-year-old man has sudden severe testicular pain. The testicle is high-riding and the cremasteric reflex is absent. What is the next best step?
rationale.rationale: Testicular torsion = surgical emergency. Do NOT delay for imaging if clinical suspicion is high. 6-hour window for salvage. Absent cremasteric reflex = most reliable sign.

rationale.text: A 48-year-old man describes the worst headache of his life that started suddenly while lifting weights. Neurologic exam is normal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise. Dx: CT head → if negative → LP for xanthochromia. Cause: ruptured berry aneurysm.

rationale.text: A 45-year-old obese woman has RUQ pain after eating a fatty meal, fever 38.2°C, and positive Murphy sign. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Acute cholecystitis: RUQ pain + fever + Murphy sign + triggered by fatty meal. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: IV fluids + antibiotics + cholecystectomy.

rationale.text: A 35-year-old man has sudden severe left flank pain radiating to the groin, hematuria, and nausea. Pain is colicky. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Renal colic: colicky flank pain radiating to groin + hematuria. Dx: CT KUB. Tx: NSAIDs + IV fluids + tamsulosin. <5mm: 90% pass spontaneously.

rationale.text: A 5-week-old male infant has projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feeding. He appears hungry after vomiting. An olive-shaped mass is felt in epigastrium. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Pyloric stenosis: projectile non-bilious vomiting at 2-6 weeks + olive mass + hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. First-born males. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

rationale.text: A 5-year-old child has high fever, drooling, dysphagia, and sits in a tripod position. He looks toxic and has inspiratory stridor. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Epiglottitis: drooling + dysphagia + tripod + toxic = AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Secure airway FIRST. Do NOT examine throat. IV ceftriaxone after airway secured.

rationale.text: A 60-year-old man has black tarry stools and lightheadedness. He takes ibuprofen daily. Melena indicates bleeding from which location?
rationale.rationale: Melena = digested blood = upper GI source above ligament of Treitz. NSAID use → peptic ulcer = #1 cause. Dx: urgent endoscopy. Tx: PPI + endoscopic hemostasis.

rationale.text: A 58-year-old man has chest pain that occurs only with exertion and is relieved completely by rest within 5 minutes. ECG normal at rest. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Stable angina: predictable chest pain with exertion, relieved by rest or nitrates. Fixed coronary stenosis. Dx: stress test. Tx: beta-blocker + nitrates + aspirin.

rationale.text: A 50-year-old alcoholic has epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. Serum lipase is 4x upper limit of normal. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back + lipase >3x ULN. Causes: Gallstones #1, Alcohol #2. Tx: IV fluids (aggressive), NPO, analgesia.

rationale.text: A 28-year-old woman has weight loss, palpitations, tremor, diarrhea, and heat intolerance. Exam shows exophthalmos and diffuse goiter. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Graves disease: most common hyperthyroidism. Exophthalmos = pathognomonic. Low TSH + high T3/T4. Tx: Methimazole first-line.

rationale.text: A 40-year-old man has central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, purple abdominal striae, and hypertension. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Cushing syndrome: excess cortisol. Most common cause: exogenous steroids. Endogenous: ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease).

rationale.text: A 42-year-old woman has symmetric joint pain/swelling in MCP and PIP joints with morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. RF and anti-CCP positive. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: RA: symmetric MCP/PIP (NOT DIP) + morning stiffness >1h + positive RF/anti-CCP. Tx: Methotrexate (DMARD) first-line.

rationale.text: A 55-year-old man wakes at 2 AM with severe pain, redness, and swelling of the first MTP joint. Serum uric acid is elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Gout: podagra (1st MTP) + hyperuricemia + negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Acute Tx: NSAIDs or colchicine. Prevention: allopurinol.

rationale.text: A 65-year-old male smoker presents with painless hematuria. No dysuria or fever. Urine culture is negative. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Painless hematuria in older smoker = bladder cancer until proven otherwise. Transitional cell carcinoma #1. Dx: Cystoscopy + urine cytology.

rationale.text: A 70-year-old woman develops sudden right arm weakness, facial droop, and inability to speak starting 1 hour ago. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Ischemic stroke: sudden focal neurologic deficit. CT head first (rule out hemorrhage). tPA if ischemic + within 4.5h. FAST: Face Arm Speech Time.

rationale.text: A 25-year-old man has progressive ascending leg weakness 3 weeks after a GI infection. Reflexes are absent. CSF shows elevated protein with normal WBC. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: GBS: ascending paralysis + areflexia + albuminocytologic dissociation after infection. Campylobacter #1 trigger. Tx: IVIG or plasmapheresis.

rationale.text: A 75-year-old woman has progressive loss of central vision and cannot read or recognize faces. Peripheral vision is intact. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: AMD: central vision loss (macula) in elderly. Dry more common. Wet (neovascular): anti-VEGF injections. Risk: age, smoking, family history.

rationale.text: A 60-year-old man has gradual painless loss of peripheral (tunnel) vision. IOP is elevated on exam. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Glaucoma: increased IOP → optic nerve damage → peripheral (tunnel) vision loss. Open-angle most common. Tx: prostaglandin eye drops (latanoprost).

rationale.text: A 3-year-old child has fever >5 days, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, rash, and swollen hands/feet. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Kawasaki disease: fever >5 days + 4/5 CRASH criteria. Tx: IVIG + aspirin. Most feared complication: coronary artery aneurysm. Screen with echocardiography.

rationale.text: A 10-month-old infant has episodic severe abdominal pain, draws up legs, vomits, and passes currant jelly stool. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Intussusception: colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stool. Age 6mo-3yr. Dx: Ultrasound (target sign). Tx: air enema reduction; surgery if failed.

rationale.text: A 32-week pregnant woman has painful vaginal bleeding and a rigid tender uterus. Fetal heart rate is abnormal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Placental abruption: PAINFUL bleeding + rigid uterus + fetal distress. Premature separation of placenta. Risk: HTN, trauma, cocaine. Tx: immediate delivery if fetal distress.

rationale.text: A 30-week pregnant woman has painless bright red vaginal bleeding. No contractions or trauma. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Placenta previa: PAINLESS bright red bleeding. Placenta covers cervical os. Do NOT do digital exam. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: C-section if severe or at term.

rationale.text: A 64-year-old man has progressive shortness of breath, orthopnea, bilateral lung crackles, and pitting leg edema. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: CHF: dyspnea + orthopnea + bilateral crackles + peripheral edema + elevated BNP = fluid overload from impaired cardiac pumping. Tx: diuretics + ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker.

rationale.text: Which medication reduces mortality in systolic heart failure?
rationale.rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality by decreasing afterload and preventing cardiac remodeling. Diuretics relieve symptoms only. Also: beta-blockers and spironolactone reduce mortality.

rationale.text: A patient has tearing chest pain radiating to the back and BP difference between arms. What is the best initial imaging?
rationale.rationale: CT angiography is fast, highly sensitive, and gold standard for diagnosing aortic dissection. CXR may show widened mediastinum but not diagnostic.

rationale.text: A 70-year-old smoker presents with hemoptysis, weight loss, and a 3 cm spiculated lung mass on CXR. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Lung cancer: smoker + hemoptysis + weight loss + spiculated mass. Adenocarcinoma is now #1 type overall. Small cell: central, highly aggressive, paraneoplastic syndromes.

rationale.text: Which type of lung cancer is most common overall?
rationale.rationale: Adenocarcinoma is now #1 lung cancer overall (peripheral location, non-smokers too). Squamous cell = central, cavitating. Small cell = most aggressive, paraneoplastic.

rationale.text: What is the recommended lung cancer screening test for high-risk patients (age 50-80, heavy smokers)?
rationale.rationale: Low-dose CT scan annually: recommended for age 50-80 with 20 pack-year history currently smoking or quit <15 years. CXR is NOT adequate for screening.

rationale.text: A 25-year-old man has RLQ pain migrating from periumbilical region, fever 38.3°C, and rebound tenderness at McBurney point. WBC 13,000. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Appendicitis: periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ + rebound at McBurney point + fever + leukocytosis = Alvarado score. Tx: appendectomy (laparoscopic preferred).

rationale.text: Which physical finding is most classic for acute appendicitis?
rationale.rationale: McBurney point: 1/3 of way from ASIS to umbilicus. Rovsing sign: RLQ pain with LLQ palpation. Murphy sign = cholecystitis. Cullen/Grey Turner = hemorrhagic pancreatitis.

rationale.text: A 60-year-old woman has severe RUQ pain, fever 39°C, and jaundice (Charcot triad). Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Charcot triad: RUQ pain + fever + jaundice = ascending cholangitis (bacterial infection of bile duct). Reynolds pentad adds hypotension + AMS. Tx: IV antibiotics + ERCP.

rationale.text: A 72-year-old man has sudden right-sided weakness. What percentage of strokes are ischemic?
rationale.rationale: 85% of strokes are ischemic (thrombotic or embolic). 15% hemorrhagic. CT head distinguishes them: ischemic = normal early; hemorrhagic = hyperdense (white) blood.

rationale.text: What is the most important modifiable risk factor for stroke?
rationale.rationale: Hypertension = #1 modifiable risk factor for stroke. Controlling BP reduces stroke risk by 40%. Other major risks: AF, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia.

rationale.text: A patient presents with polyuria, polydipsia, and random blood glucose of 14 mmol/L. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: Diabetes mellitus: polyuria + polydipsia + hyperglycemia. Random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L = diagnostic if symptomatic. Or: fasting ≥7.0, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or 2h OGTT ≥11.1.

rationale.text: Which single test is most commonly used to diagnose AND monitor diabetes?
rationale.rationale: HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes. Reflects average blood glucose over 3 months. Used for both diagnosis and monitoring. Target in treated diabetics: <7%.

rationale.text: What is the first-line pharmacotherapy for newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes without cardiovascular disease?
rationale.rationale: Metformin: first-line for T2DM. Reduces hepatic glucose production, no hypoglycemia, weight neutral, cheap. With CVD or CKD: add SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA.

rationale.text: A 20-year-old presents with sore throat, fever, and white tonsillar exudate. Rapid strep test is positive. Most likely organism?
rationale.rationale: Group A Strep pharyngitis: white exudate + fever + no cough + tender anterior cervical nodes (Centor criteria). Tx: penicillin or amoxicillin x10 days to prevent rheumatic fever.

rationale.text: What is the first-line antibiotic for streptococcal pharyngitis?
rationale.rationale: Penicillin V x10 days = gold standard for Group A strep pharyngitis. Amoxicillin acceptable. Azithromycin if penicillin allergy. Goal: prevent acute rheumatic fever.

rationale.text: A 35-year-old immigrant presents with 3-week cough, fever, night sweats, and 8 kg weight loss. CXR shows upper lobe cavitary lesion. Diagnosis?
rationale.rationale: TB: cough >3 weeks + fever + night sweats + weight loss + upper lobe cavitary lesion. Dx: sputum AFB smear/culture + CXR. Tx: RIPE x2 months then RI x4 months.

rationale.text: A 4-year-old child has barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and mild respiratory distress. Diagnosis and first-line treatment?
rationale.rationale: Croup: barking cough + stridor. Parainfluenza virus. Mild: oral dexamethasone. Moderate-severe: add racemic epinephrine nebulization. Epiglottitis: drooling + toxic appearance.

rationale.text: What is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants?
rationale.rationale: RSV = Respiratory Syncytial Virus = #1 cause of bronchiolitis in infants <2 years. Treatment: supportive care (hydration + O₂). Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk infants.

rationale.text: Which neurotransmitter is primarily implicated in the pathophysiology of major depressive disorder?
rationale.rationale: Serotonin deficiency is the primary neurotransmitter hypothesis of depression. SSRIs work by blocking serotonin reuptake. Also: norepinephrine and dopamine involved.

rationale.text: A 28-year-old has recurrent unexpected panic attacks with palpitations, sweating, and fear of dying. Cardiac workup is normal. First-line treatment?
rationale.rationale: Panic disorder first-line: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) + CBT. Benzodiazepines for SHORT-TERM acute attacks only (risk of dependence). Beta-blockers for performance anxiety.

rationale.text: A patient is having an acute severe panic attack in the ER. Which drug provides the fastest relief?
rationale.rationale: Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam, alprazolam) provide rapid relief of acute anxiety/panic. Onset minutes. NOT for long-term use. SSRIs are for long-term prevention.

rationale.text: A 52-year-old woman comes for routine screening. She smokes 1 pack/day for 30 years. Which lung cancer screening is recommended?
rationale.rationale: Annual low-dose CT scan for age 50-80 with ≥20 pack-year history. Reduces lung cancer mortality by ~20%. CXR is NOT recommended for screening.

rationale.text: A competent 55-year-old Jehovah's Witness refuses a blood transfusion needed for surgery. He understands he may die. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Respect refusal, document, proceed without transfusion
rationale.rationale: Competent adult autonomy: right to refuse ANY treatment, including life-saving. Document capacity assessment and refusal. Explore alternatives (cell salvage, erythropoietin). Family cannot override capable adult.

rationale.text: A physician accidentally prescribes the wrong dose of a medication. The patient takes it but suffers no harm. What is the physician's obligation?
options: No disclosure needed since no harm
rationale.rationale: Canadian ethics: full disclosure of adverse events and near-misses is mandatory, even if no harm occurred. Apology is not legal admission of liability (Ontario Apology Act). Transparency builds trust.
